info.name: Symfony API
info.description:
# Symfony API — Complete Reference

## Overview

The **Symfony API** is a RESTful web service built on the [Symfony PHP framework](https://symfony.com/), one of the most mature and widely adopted PHP frameworks in the world. It is designed to expose structured application data over HTTP in a predictable, stateless, and machine-readable format. This collection documents every available endpoint in the API, providing everything a developer needs to integrate, test, debug, and extend the service.

REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for distributed hypermedia systems. In a RESTful API:
- Each **URL** identifies a unique resource (e.g., `/api/categories` identifies the collection of all categories).
- **HTTP methods** define the action: `GET` retrieves data, `POST` creates, `PUT`/`PATCH` updates, `DELETE` removes.
- **Responses are stateless**: every request contains all the information the server needs; no session state is stored between requests.
- **Responses are self-contained**: the response body, status code, and headers together fully describe the outcome.

This API is currently configured for **local development** and runs on your machine at `http://127.0.0.1:8000`. All endpoints are prefixed with `/api/` to namespace them away from any web UI routes that Symfony may also serve.

---

## Base URL

```
http://127.0.0.1:8000
```

All endpoint paths in this collection are relative to this base URL. For example, the full URL for the categories endpoint is:

```
http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories
```

To avoid hardcoding this value in every request, create a Postman environment variable named `base_url` with the value `http://127.0.0.1:8000`, then reference it in requests as `{{base_url}}/api/categories`.

---

## Available Endpoints

| Method | Path | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `GET` | `/api/categories` | Retrieve a list of all categories |

> More endpoints will be added as the API grows. Each new endpoint will be documented in this collection with the same level of detail.

---

## Required Request Headers

Every request to this API **must** include the following HTTP headers. Omitting them may result in unexpected responses, including HTML error pages instead of JSON.

### `Accept: application/json`

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Header name** | `Accept` |
| **Header value** | `application/json` |
| **Required** | Yes, for all requests |

The `Accept` header tells the server what media type the client can understand. When set to `application/json`, Symfony's serializer and error handler will return all responses — including error responses — as JSON objects rather than HTML pages. Without this header, a `404 Not Found` or `500 Internal Server Error` will return a full Symfony HTML debug page, which is not useful for programmatic clients.

**How to add it in Postman:**
1. Open the request.
2. Click the **Headers** tab.
3. Add a new row: Key = `Accept`, Value = `application/json`.
4. Make sure the checkbox on the left is ticked (enabled).

### `Content-Type: application/json`

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Header name** | `Content-Type` |
| **Header value** | `application/json` |
| **Required** | Yes, for `POST`, `PUT`, and `PATCH` requests |

The `Content-Type` header tells the server the format of the request body you are sending. For `GET` requests (like Get Categories), this header is not needed because there is no request body. For any future `POST` or `PUT` endpoints, you must include this header alongside `Accept: application/json`.

---

## JSON Response Structure

All successful responses from this API return JSON. The structure depends on the endpoint, but all resources in this API share a common set of fields.

### Category Object

A **category** is the primary resource exposed by this API. Each category object has the following fields:

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `id` | `integer` | No | The unique numeric identifier for this category. Assigned automatically by the database when the category is created. This value is immutable — it never changes after creation. Use this field to reference a specific category in other API calls (e.g., `GET /api/categories/{id}`). |
| `name` | `string` | No | The human-readable display name of the category. This is the label shown to end users. It is a plain UTF-8 string with no maximum length enforced at the API level (though the database may impose one). |

### Collection Response (Array of Categories)

Endpoints that return multiple resources (like `GET /api/categories`) return a **JSON array** at the top level. Each element of the array is a category object as described above.

**Example response with data:**

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "name": "Books"
  },
  {
    "id": 3,
    "name": "Clothing"
  }
]
```

**Example response when no categories exist:**

```json
[]
```

An empty array `[]` is a valid, successful response. It means the database has no category records yet. This is not an error — the HTTP status code will still be `200 OK`.

### Field Details

#### `id` (integer)

- **Type:** Positive integer (e.g., `1`, `42`, `1000`)
- **Source:** Auto-incremented primary key in the database
- **Immutable:** Yes — once assigned, this value never changes
- **Uniqueness:** Guaranteed unique across all categories
- **Use case:** Use this value to construct URLs for single-resource endpoints, e.g., `GET /api/categories/1`
- **Never zero or negative** in a real database

#### `name` (string)

- **Type:** UTF-8 string
- **Source:** Set when the category is created or updated
- **Mutable:** Yes — can be changed via `PUT` or `PATCH` endpoints
- **Uniqueness:** Not guaranteed unless enforced by the database schema
- **Use case:** Display to end users in dropdowns, filters, navigation menus, etc.
- **May contain:** Spaces, accented characters, Unicode (e.g., `"Électronique"`, `"Bücher"`)

---

## HTTP Status Codes

This API uses standard HTTP status codes to communicate the outcome of every request. Always check the status code before reading the response body.

### 200 OK

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `200` |
| **Meaning** | The request was received, understood, and processed successfully |
| **Response body** | JSON data as described in the endpoint documentation |

You will receive a `200 OK` when:
- You call `GET /api/categories` and the server successfully queries the database
- The result may be a full array of categories or an empty array `[]` — both are `200 OK`

**Example:**
```json
[
  { "id": 1, "name": "Electronics" },
  { "id": 2, "name": "Books" }
]
```

**What to do:** Read the response body and use the data.

---

### 404 Not Found

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `404` |
| **Meaning** | The requested URL does not match any route registered in Symfony |
| **Response body** | JSON error object (if `Accept: application/json` is set) or HTML page |

You will receive a `404 Not Found` when:
- You mistype the URL (e.g., `/api/category` instead of `/api/categories`)
- You request a resource by ID that does not exist (e.g., `GET /api/categories/9999` when ID 9999 is not in the database)
- The route exists in code but the Symfony router cache is stale

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-10",
  "title": "An error occurred",
  "detail": "No route found for \"GET /api/category\"",
  "status": 404
}
```

**What to do:**
1. Double-check the URL path for typos.
2. Run `php bin/console debug:router` in your terminal to list all registered routes.
3. If you recently added a new route, clear the Symfony cache: `php bin/console cache:clear`.

---

### 500 Internal Server Error

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `500` |
| **Meaning** | The server encountered an unexpected condition that prevented it from fulfilling the request |
| **Response body** | JSON error object (if `Accept: application/json` is set) or HTML debug page |

You will receive a `500 Internal Server Error` when:
- The database is not running or the connection credentials in `.env` are wrong
- There is a PHP exception in the controller or service layer
- A required Symfony service is misconfigured
- The database schema is out of sync with the entity (run `php bin/console doctrine:migrations:migrate`)

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-10",
  "title": "An error occurred",
  "detail": "An exception occurred in the driver: SQLSTATE[HY000] [2002] Connection refused",
  "status": 500
}
```

**What to do:**
1. Check that your database server (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, etc.) is running.
2. Verify the `DATABASE_URL` value in your `.env` or `.env.local` file.
3. Open the Symfony profiler at `http://127.0.0.1:8000/_profiler` to see the full exception trace.
4. Check `var/log/dev.log` for detailed error messages.
5. Run `php bin/console doctrine:schema:validate` to check for schema mismatches.

---

## Usage Guidelines

### 1. Start the Symfony Development Server

Before sending any request, make sure the Symfony server is running:

```bash
# Using the Symfony CLI (recommended)
symfony server:start

# Or using PHP's built-in server
php -S 127.0.0.1:8000 -t public/
```

The server must be running at `http://127.0.0.1:8000` for all requests in this collection to work.

### 2. Always Include the Accept Header

Every request must include `Accept: application/json`. This ensures:
- All responses (including errors) are returned as JSON
- You can programmatically parse both success and error responses
- You avoid receiving Symfony's HTML debug pages in your Postman response viewer

### 3. Use a Postman Environment for the Base URL

Instead of hardcoding `http://127.0.0.1:8000` in every request:
1. Go to **Environments** in the left sidebar.
2. Create a new environment called `Symfony Local`.
3. Add a variable: Name = `base_url`, Initial Value = `http://127.0.0.1:8000`, Current Value = `http://127.0.0.1:8000`.
4. Save the environment and select it from the environment dropdown (top-right of Postman).
5. Update all request URLs to use `{{base_url}}/api/...`.

This makes it easy to switch between local, staging, and production environments by simply changing the active environment.

### 4. Understand the Status Code Before Reading the Body

Always check the HTTP status code in Postman's response panel before interpreting the body:
- `200` → success, read the data
- `404` → wrong URL or missing resource, fix the path
- `500` → server-side error, check logs

### 5. Check Symfony Logs for Errors

When you receive a `500` error, the most useful debugging tool is the Symfony log file:

```bash
tail -f var/log/dev.log
```

This streams new log entries in real time as you send requests from Postman.

### 6. Database Setup

If you are running this API for the first time, make sure the database is created and migrations are applied:

```bash
php bin/console doctrine:database:create
php bin/console doctrine:migrations:migrate
```

Without this, all endpoints that query the database will return `500 Internal Server Error`.

---

## Getting Started Checklist

- [ ] Symfony development server is running at `http://127.0.0.1:8000`
- [ ] Database is running and `DATABASE_URL` is set correctly in `.env`
- [ ] Migrations have been applied (`doctrine:migrations:migrate`)
- [ ] Postman environment `Symfony Local` is created with `base_url = http://127.0.0.1:8000`
- [ ] All requests include the `Accept: application/json` header
- [ ] You have sent `GET /api/categories` and received a `200 OK` response

request.description:
# Get Categories

Retrieves a complete list of all category resources stored in the database. This is a **read-only** endpoint — it does not create, modify, or delete any data. The response is a JSON array where each element represents one category object.

---

## Endpoint

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Method** | `GET` |
| **URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Authentication** | None required |
| **Request Body** | None |
| **Idempotent** | Yes — calling this endpoint multiple times always returns the same result without side effects |

---

## Required Request Headers

The following headers must be included with every request to this endpoint.

| Header | Value | Required | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Accept` | `application/json` | **Yes** | Instructs the server to return the response as JSON. Without this header, Symfony may return an HTML page instead of structured data — especially for error responses like 404 and 500. |

**How to add this header in Postman:**

1. Open this request in Postman.
2. Click the **Headers** tab (next to Params, Authorization, Body).
3. In an empty row, enter:
   - **Key:** `Accept`
   - **Value:** `application/json`
4. Make sure the checkbox on the left side of the row is **checked** (enabled).
5. Click **Send**.

> `Content-Type` is **not** required for this request because `GET` requests do not have a request body. Only include `Content-Type: application/json` on requests that send a body (POST, PUT, PATCH).

---

## Query Parameters

This endpoint does not accept any query parameters. The full list of categories is always returned. Filtering, sorting, and pagination are not currently implemented.

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *(none)* | — | — | No query parameters are supported |

---

## JSON Response Structure

A successful `200 OK` response returns a **JSON array** at the top level. Each element of the array is a **category object**.

### Category Object Fields

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `id` | `integer` | No | The unique numeric identifier for this category. Auto-assigned by the database. Immutable after creation. |
| `name` | `string` | No | The human-readable display name of the category. UTF-8 encoded. |

### `id` — Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** Positive integer (e.g., `1`, `7`, `42`)
- **Assigned by:** The database engine automatically, using an auto-increment primary key
- **Immutable:** Yes — this value is set once at creation and never changes, even if the category's `name` is updated
- **Unique:** Guaranteed to be unique across all category records
- **Never zero or negative** in a real database
- **Primary use:** Reference this value when building URLs for single-resource endpoints, e.g.:
  ```
  GET /api/categories/1
  PUT /api/categories/1
  DELETE /api/categories/1
  ```
- **In your front-end:** Store this value as the key in dropdowns, lists, and state management so you can identify which category the user selected

### `name` — Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string
- **Set by:** The application when a category is created or updated
- **Mutable:** Yes — can be changed via `PUT` or `PATCH` endpoints
- **Uniqueness:** Not guaranteed at the API level (depends on database constraints)
- **May contain:** Spaces, punctuation, accented characters, Unicode (e.g., `"Électronique"`, `"Bücher"`, `"Категорія"`)
- **Primary use:** Display to end users in navigation menus, filter dropdowns, product listings, etc.

### Full Response Example (with data)

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "name": "Books"
  },
  {
    "id": 3,
    "name": "Clothing"
  },
  {
    "id": 4,
    "name": "Home & Garden"
  }
]
```

### Empty Response Example

```json
[]
```

An empty array is a **valid, successful response**. It means the `categories` table in the database exists but contains no rows. The HTTP status code will still be `200 OK`. This is not an error — do not treat it as one in your application code.

---

## HTTP Status Codes

### 200 OK

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `200` |
| **Meaning** | Request succeeded. The server queried the database and returned the result. |
| **When you see it** | The Symfony server is running, the database is reachable, and the route is correctly registered |

**Example response:**
```json
[
  { "id": 1, "name": "Electronics" },
  { "id": 2, "name": "Books" }
]
```

**What to do:** Read the array and use the data. If the array is empty (`[]`), it means no categories have been created yet — this is still a success.

---

### 404 Not Found

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `404` |
| **Meaning** | The URL you requested does not match any route registered in Symfony |
| **When you see it** | URL typo, wrong path, or Symfony route cache is stale |

**Common causes for this endpoint:**
- Typing `/api/category` instead of `/api/categories` (missing the `s`)
- Typing `/api/Categories` (wrong case — URLs are case-sensitive)
- The Symfony server is running but the route was not registered (missing annotation/attribute or YAML config)
- The route cache is outdated after a code change

**Example response body (when `Accept: application/json` is set):**
```json
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-10",
  "title": "An error occurred",
  "detail": "No route found for \"GET /api/category\"",
  "status": 404
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Verify the exact URL: `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories`
2. Run this command to list all registered routes and confirm the path:
   ```bash
   php bin/console debug:router
   ```
3. If you recently changed route configuration, clear the cache:
   ```bash
   php bin/console cache:clear
   ```

---

### 500 Internal Server Error

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `500` |
| **Meaning** | The server encountered an unexpected error while processing the request |
| **When you see it** | Database connection failure, PHP exception in the controller, misconfigured service |

**Common causes for this endpoint:**
- The database server (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite) is not running
- The `DATABASE_URL` in `.env` or `.env.local` is incorrect
- The `categories` table does not exist (migrations not run)
- A PHP exception is thrown inside the `CategoryController`
- Symfony's service container is misconfigured

**Example response body (when `Accept: application/json` is set):**
```json
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-10",
  "title": "An error occurred",
  "detail": "An exception occurred in the driver: SQLSTATE[HY000] [2002] Connection refused",
  "status": 500
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Confirm your database server is running.
2. Check `DATABASE_URL` in `.env`:
   ```
   DATABASE_URL="mysql://root:password@127.0.0.1:3306/my_database"
   ```
3. Run pending migrations:
   ```bash
   php bin/console doctrine:migrations:migrate
   ```
4. Open the Symfony profiler for a full stack trace:
   ```
   http://127.0.0.1:8000/_profiler
   ```
5. Tail the Symfony log file in real time:
   ```bash
   tail -f var/log/dev.log
   ```

---

## Usage Guidelines

### Sending the Request from JavaScript (fetch)

```javascript
const response = await fetch('http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories', {
  method: 'GET',
  headers: {
    'Accept': 'application/json'
  }
});

if (!response.ok) {
  throw new Error(`HTTP error: ${response.status}`);
}

const categories = await response.json();
console.log(categories);
// Output: [{ id: 1, name: 'Electronics' }, { id: 2, name: 'Books' }, ...]
```

### Using the `id` Field in Subsequent Requests

The `id` returned by this endpoint is the key you use to interact with individual categories in other endpoints:

```
GET    /api/categories/1      → fetch a single category
PUT    /api/categories/1      → update a category's name
DELETE /api/categories/1      → delete a category
```

Always use the `id` from the API response — never hardcode IDs in your application, as they may differ between development, staging, and production databases.

### Handling an Empty Array

If the response is `[]`, do not show an error to the user. Instead, show an empty state message:

```javascript
if (categories.length === 0) {
  console.log('No categories found. Create one to get started.');
} else {
  // render the list
}
```

### Postman Test Scripts

Add these to the **Scripts → Post-response** tab of this request to automatically validate the response every time you send it:

```javascript
pm.test('Status code is 200', function () {
  pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

pm.test('Response is a JSON array', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.be.an('array');
});

pm.test('Each category has id (number) and name (string)', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  body.forEach(function (category) {
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('id').that.is.a('number');
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('name').that.is.a('string');
  });
});
```

---

## Prerequisites Checklist

Before sending this request, confirm:

- [ ] Symfony server is running: `symfony server:start` or `php -S 127.0.0.1:8000 -t public/`
- [ ] Database server is running and accessible
- [ ] `DATABASE_URL` is correctly set in `.env` or `.env.local`
- [ ] Migrations have been applied: `php bin/console doctrine:migrations:migrate`
- [ ] The `Accept: application/json` header is present and enabled in the Headers tab
- [ ] The correct Postman environment is selected (if using `{{base_url}}`)
